flashcards.front: What is a function in discrete mathematics?
flashcards.back: A function f from set A (domain) to set B (codomain) is a relation where each element of A is associated with exactly one element of B. Denoted f: A → B.

flashcards.front: Define the domain of a function.
flashcards.back: The domain of a function f is the set of all possible input values (first components) for which the function is defined.

flashcards.front: Define the codomain of a function.
flashcards.back: The codomain is the set B into which all outputs of the function are constrained to fall. It contains the range as a subset.

flashcards.front: Define the range (image) of a function.
flashcards.back: The range is the set of all actual output values produced by the function. It is a subset of the codomain.
A function f: A → B is one-to-one if different inputs always produce different outputs. If f(a₁) = f(a₂), then a₁ = a₂.
A function f: A → B is onto if every element in the codomain B has at least one preimage in the domain A. Range = Codomain.
A function that is both one-to-one (injective) and onto (surjective). Every element in the codomain has exactly one preimage.

flashcards.front: What is the horizontal line test for one-to-one functions?
flashcards.back: If any horizontal line intersects the graph of a function at most once, the function is one-to-one. Multiple intersections indicate it's not one-to-one.

flashcards.front: Define the composition of two functions.
flashcards.back: Given f: A → B and g: B → C, the composition (g ∘ f): A → C is defined as (g ∘ f)(x) = g(f(x)) for all x ∈ A.
The identity function Iₙ: A → A defined by Iₙ(x) = x for all x ∈ A. It maps every element to itself.

flashcards.front: When does a function have an inverse?
flashcards.back: A function f has an inverse f⁻¹ if and only if f is bijective (one-to-one and onto). The inverse reverses the mapping.
A function f: A → B where all elements of A map to a single element c in B. f(x) = c for all x ∈ A.
A plain function (or arbitrary function) is any function from domain to codomain without restrictions - can be one-to-one, onto, or neither.
The floor function maps a real number x to the greatest integer less than or equal to x. Example: ⌊3.7⌋ = 3, ⌊-2.3⌋ = -3.
The ceiling function maps a real number x to the smallest integer greater than or equal to x. Example: ⌈3.2⌉ = 4, ⌈-2.3⌉ = -2.
